figures.name: The Loka (Cosmic Man)
figures.gloss: The whole universe shaped like a vast man standing arms-akimbo — the hells stacked in his legs, the middle world at his waist, the heavens in his chest, and the liberated souls a crescent at the crown of his head. It was never made and will never end.
figures.facet: The precious counter-shard — a cosmos with NO creator: the Loka is eternal, uncaused, self-existent, neither born from a sky-father nor hatched from an egg nor spoken into being. Where every other tradition seats a maker at the centre of its axis, Jainism puts only impersonal substance and law, and dares the universe to need no god at all.
figures.provenance: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti, c. 2nd–5th c. CE) and the Lokaprakāśa cosmographies; the anthropomorphic loka-puruṣa is a later iconographic systematisation of the older three-world scheme.

figures.name: Kāla-cakra (The Wheel of Time)
figures.gloss: Time as an ever-turning wheel of twelve spokes — six ages descending from a golden ease into a leaden misery (avasarpiṇī), then six ascending back up (utsarpiṇī) — grinding round forever without beginning or final end.
figures.facet: Decline and renewal with no destroyer and no judgment: the ages worsen and mend by the wheel's own turning, not by a god's wrath or a saviour's return. Even the Tīrthaṅkaras can appear only in certain spokes — the cosmic order outranks every teacher who arises within it.
figures.provenance: Śvetāmbara and Digambara cosmologies; the avasarpiṇī/utsarpiṇī scheme is canonical across the Tattvārtha and Āgama traditions.

figures.name: The Twenty-Four Tīrthaṅkaras
figures.gloss: The ford-makers — the recurring series of fully liberated teachers who, in each descending half-cycle, build the 'ford' (tīrtha) across the river of rebirth by re-founding the four-fold community and re-teaching the eternal law. Twenty-four arise in each cycle, the same office filled again and again.
figures.facet: One pattern, twenty-four faces, no god among them: the Tīrthaṅkaras are not avatars of a deity nor incarnations of one being but separate souls who each independently reached the same summit. The 'one-through-many' here is not one essence refracted but one ATTAINMENT repeated — a democracy of liberation, not an emanation.
figures.provenance: Listed in the Kalpa Sūtra and Tattvārtha tradition; Ṛṣabha first, Pārśva (23rd) and Mahāvīra (24th) historically attested c. 8th and 6th–5th c. BCE.

figures.name: Ṛṣabhanātha (Ādinātha)
figures.gloss: The first Tīrthaṅkara of this cycle — the Bull-lord who, when the wish-granting trees of the golden age failed, taught humankind agriculture, fire, crafts, writing, law and kingship before renouncing it all to win liberation.
figures.facet: The benefactor who then walks away: Ṛṣabha gives civilisation not as a stolen gift nor a divine decree but as a stage to be outgrown — the culture-hero whose final teaching is that the arts he founded are themselves a bondage to renounce.
figures.provenance: Kalpa Sūtra and Ādi Purāṇa (Jinasena, 9th c. CE); his teaching of the 'six occupations' is the Jain civilisational origin-myth.

figures.name: Mahāvīra
figures.gloss: The twenty-fourth and last Tīrthaṅkara of this descending age — the historical Vardhamāna, contemporary of the Buddha, who through extreme asceticism conquered all passion (jina, 'victor') and re-proclaimed the eternal teaching of non-violence and the many-sidedness of truth.
figures.facet: A founder who founds nothing new: Mahāvīra insists he only re-teaches what Pārśva and the twenty-two before him taught and the next cycle's Tīrthaṅkaras will teach again — authority by re-discovery of an eternal law, never by revelation or invention.
figures.provenance: Ācārāṅga and Kalpa Sūtra; the only Tīrthaṅkara with firm historical footing alongside Pārśva (c. 599–527 BCE by traditional dating).

figures.name: Jīva (The Soul-Substance)
figures.gloss: The countless eternal souls — each uncreated, each intrinsically all-knowing and blissful, each weighed down only by the karmic matter (ajīva) clinging to it. Every living thing, down to a drop of water or a flame, houses a jīva straining toward release.
figures.facet: Infinite souls, no over-soul: against the Vedic Ātman dissolving into one Brahman, Jainism holds the souls eternally PLURAL and distinct even in liberation. Reality is the standing tension of two substances, soul and non-soul — a dualism of spirit and matter rather than good versus evil.
figures.provenance: Tattvārtha Sūtra; the jīva/ajīva division and karmic-matter doctrine are foundational Jain metaphysics, explicitly counter-Upaniṣadic.

figures.name: Ahiṃsā (Non-Harm)
figures.gloss: The supreme vow — to injure no living thing in thought, word or deed, since every creature down to the unseen one-sensed beings of earth, water, fire and air bears a soul as worthy as one's own. The monk sweeps his path and strains his water; the lay-person foreswears the harming trades.
figures.facet: Law kept by restraint, not sacrifice: where neighbouring cults bound the cosmos with blood-offering, Jainism binds it by withholding the knife entirely. The right-order of the world is upheld by what one refuses to do — non-action as the highest rite.
figures.provenance: Ācārāṅga Sūtra; ahiṃsā as the first and governing vow (mahāvrata) is the ethical core of the whole tradition.

figures.name: Anekāntavāda (Many-Sidedness)
figures.gloss: The doctrine that reality has infinite aspects and no single statement captures it whole — every truth is partial, asserted only 'from a standpoint' (syādvāda), so the blind men and the elephant are all correct and all incomplete.
figures.facet: Complementarity raised to an epistemology: where Yin-Yang or Qian-Kun balance two cosmic powers, anekāntavāda multiplies the standpoints to infinity and makes humility itself the law of knowing — the refusal of any final word as a spiritual discipline.
figures.provenance: Systematised by Kundakunda and Siddhasena Divākara (c. 5th c. CE); syādvāda (the 'sevenfold predication') is its logical form.

figures.name: The Siddha-loka (Crown of Liberated Souls)
figures.gloss: The crescent at the very summit of the cosmic man, just below the roof of the universe, where every liberated soul rises and rests forever — omniscient, bodiless, passionless, each keeping its own individuality while sharing one identical state of bliss.
figures.facet: Liberation without merger and without a god to greet it: the freed soul does not join a deity or dissolve into an absolute — it simply rises to the ceiling of the world and abides, self-sufficient. The 'one-through-many' completed as countless equals, not as a return to the One.
figures.provenance: Tattvārtha Sūtra and Jain cosmography; the soul's upward motion to the siddha-śilā at liberation is canonical.

edges.note: The same uncreated cosmos seen in space and in time: the eternal Loka endures while the Wheel of Time turns within it — two faces of one beginningless, makerless universe.
The ford-makers are scheduled BY the wheel — twenty-four arise per descending half-cycle and none can appear outside their appointed spokes; the office is a function of cyclic time itself.
The first ford-maker of this cycle — Ṛṣabha opens the series of twenty-four, the one-through-many begun.
The last ford-maker of this cycle — Mahāvīra closes the series Ṛṣabha opened, re-teaching what all twenty-four eternally teach.
Because every jīva is an eternal soul as precious as one's own, harming any is the gravest karmic bondage — ahiṃsā is the direct ethical consequence of the soul-doctrine.
The same soul before and after: the jīva burdened with karmic matter is the soul that, cleansed, rises to the Siddha-loka and abides forever distinct.

Sibling Indic systematisations of beginningless cyclic time — both teach worlds rising and falling by impersonal law with no creator to start the round; sister-śramaṇa traditions of the same Gangetic ferment.
Both Indic cosmologies set the lived world inside vast recurring world-ages; the Vedic yuga-cycle and the Jain kāla-cakra are parallel readings of declining and renewing time.
Two śramaṇa answers from the same age and region: both displace Vedic blood-sacrifice and a creator-god, Jainism by the vow of non-harm, Buddhism by no-self — kindred refusals, not derivations.
The sharpest fault-line between the śramaṇa siblings: Jainism makes the eternal plural soul the bedrock of reality; Buddhism denies any abiding self at all. Same problem, opposite verdict.

Against the Upaniṣadic merger of Ātman into one Brahman, Jainism insists souls stay eternally MANY and distinct even when freed — a deliberate doctrinal counter to Vedic non-dualism it grew up beside.

Both split reality into conscious spirit and unconscious matter — jīva/ajīva mirrors puruṣa/prakṛti as Indic spirit-matter dualisms, though Jainism multiplies the souls where Sāṃkhya keeps puruṣas plural too.

Jainism keeps the Indic three-world axis and the world-mountain but evicts the gods from its peak — where Meru is crowned by Brahmā's heaven, the Loka's summit holds only liberated souls and no creator anywhere.
Both śramaṇa cosmographies inherit the Meru-centred vertical world and then hollow out its theism — a makerless axis-mundi in two neighbouring traditions.
The counter-shard at its sharpest: where Vedānta makes the cosmos the play of one absolute, Jainism denies any ground-of-being — only eternal substances and law, no One behind the many.
Both arrange the cosmos vertically as worlds stacked on an axis — hells below, lived world at the middle, blessed at the top — though the Loka is a man's body where Yggdrasil is a tree.

The cosmic-man shape echoes the Vedic Puruṣa whose body becomes the world — but the Jain Loka is no one's body and is never sacrificed; the form is borrowed, the primordial-sacrifice pointedly absent.

Both bring fire and the civilising arts to a helpless humankind — but Ṛṣabha gives freely as a king and then renounces the gift, where Prometheus steals it and is punished. The culture-hero as renouncer, not rebel.
Both are the founder-king of the arts who finally departs the kingdom he taught — agriculture, writing and law given, then the throne abandoned for a higher liberation.

The same śramaṇa victory under one image: Mahāvīra the 'victor' (jina) conquers all passion by asceticism just as the Buddha sees through Māra's armies — contemporaries of one age and region, kindred conquests of desire, not one borrowed from the other.
Both crown non-action: ahiṃsā upholds the world by the knife withheld as the Dao's wu-wei upholds it by the deed unforced — right order kept by restraint rather than assertion.

Both refuse the single absolute claim — Yin-Yang by pairing every truth with its complement, anekāntavāda by multiplying standpoints to infinity; complementarity as two poles versus complementarity as countless.
Where Advaita resolves all standpoints into one undivided truth, anekāntavāda holds that no standpoint is ever final — pluralism of perspective set against non-dual unity.

Liberation as the great divider: the Jain soul rises to the world's ceiling and abides forever ITSELF, while the Vedic ātman is freed by dissolving INTO Brahman — release as persistence versus release as merger.
Both deny a creator-ground, yet the freed Jain jīva remains an eternal individual where the Buddhist nirvāṇa extinguishes the very notion of an abiding self — kindred denials, opposite destinies.
The deepest divergence in the mirror: against the sky-father at the head of nearly every pantheon, the Loka enthrones no one — an uncreated, fatherless cosmos that needs no god to hold it up.
